text:
OVERVIEW

Hollow Knight: Silksong is a Metroidvania and Soulslike that has been very popular since launch. Like the original Hollow Knight, it is not an easy game: combat can be brutal, areas are vast and full of secrets, and platforming demands precision that many similar titles avoid. This page is a directory of guides covering boss strategies, area progression walkthroughs, side quest explanations, item locations, and more, intended to help players progress toward The Citadel and beyond. It is described as a work in progress that is updated regularly.

AREA PROGRESSION GUIDES: Search up

AREA PROGRESSION GUIDES: Search up

Areas in Silksong start out manageable but quickly become more challenging in both combat and exploration. Maps are full of alternate paths, secret rooms, hidden points of interest, and collectibles, so it is easy to get turned around. The walkthroughs below explain the most efficient ways to progress through each area without missing important items and encounters.

Are you finding Hollow Knight: Silksong too easy? Did you clock it already and fancy a new challenge? Probably not, but developer Team Cherry has you covered anyway.

It turns out the game features a hidden hard mode of sorts. Steel Soul is a hardcore permadeath mode that locks you out of your save file after a single death. "No reviving. Death is permanent," reads the description.

Steel Soul mode was present in the original Hollow Knight and unlocked after beating the game once. But Team Cherry has included a secret method to unlock Silksong's Steel Soul mode at any point, as fans discovered almost immediately after the game's launch.

To unlock Steel Soul mode in Silksong, select the Extras option on the main menu. Then, when on the Extras screen, enter these commands: up, down, up, down, left, right, left, right (which is similar to, but not quite, the famous Konami code). A flash and a thudding sound will let you know the code has been recognized.

Then, when selecting a fresh game file and starting a new game, you'll be offered a choice between Classic and Steel Soul. If you die in a Steel Soul run, the save file will be marked as "Defeated." Select it and you're given the option to clear the file and start again.

It's a simple enough mode, but given the game's already notorious difficulty (which Team Cherry has already pledged to tone down slightly), it's a monumental challenge for anyone hardy enough.

How to Spot Bosses in Silksong?
Boss fights in Hollow Knight: Silksong can be easy to miss at first, but there are several clear signs that tell you when a boss is already in front of you. The most reliable sign is the name display. Whenever you face a main boss, their name will appear in the lower-left corner of the screen as soon as the fight starts. For example, if you meet an enemy and a name like Moss Mother appears in that spot, it is guaranteed to be a boss encounter.

Another key signal is the scream. Bosses always let out a loud cry before the fight begins, warning you that the battle is about to start. They often release a second scream when they are defeated or changing phases, marking the end of the fight.

Boss arenas also stand out from the rest of the game. A boss usually waits in a room or open space that feels different from the normal path you have been exploring. These areas may look larger, more decorated, or simply more complicated than the surrounding tunnels, giving you a clear hint that something important is coming.

By watching for the name popup, listening for the opening scream, and noticing a change in the environment, you can quickly tell when you are about to face one of Silksong's many challenging bosses.

Do Bosses have health bars in Silksong?
One thing that makes bosses in Hollow Knight Silksong different from those in many other action or RPG games is the lack of a visible health bar. During a boss fight, you will not see any gauge or meter showing how much life the enemy has left. Instead, you must keep attacking until the boss reacts in specific ways.

A boss will often let out a loud scream when something important happens. This scream can mean two things: either the boss has been defeated, or it is about to enter a new phase of the fight. Some bosses gain new moves or become more aggressive after this scream, so be ready to adapt.

Even without a health bar, you can still get a sense of a boss's remaining strength by watching its behavior. As the battle goes on, bosses may attack faster, strike harder, or perform more complicated attacks. These signs usually mean the fight is reaching its final moments and the boss's health is almost gone.

Do Bosses have Phases in HK Silksong?
Most bosses in Hollow Knight: Silksong fight in multiple phases, with each phase becoming more dangerous than the last. A clear sign that a boss is about to change phases is when it becomes stunned for a short moment and lets out a loud scream. This brief pause signals that the next part of the battle is about to begin.

When a boss enters a new phase, it will often speed up its existing attacks or introduce new moves to its pattern. Some bosses may even combine different attacks at the same time, creating more complex and harder-to-avoid sequences.

Knowing these signs helps you prepare for sudden shifts in the fight. If you hear the scream or see the boss stagger, stay alert because its next attacks will likely be faster, stronger, and more unpredictable.

How do Mini-Bosses Differ from Main Bosses in Silksong?
Unlike main Bosses, Mini-Boss names do not appear on the left hand side of the screen. While you may think Mini-Bosses can be easier than Main Bosses, this isn't the case. Some Mini-Bosses can be trickier to fight than main bosses. The only indicator you can have that you've encountered a Mini-Boss is when you no name appears but it shouts before the fight.

How many Bosses are in HK Silksong?
As of now, Hollow Knight: Silksong features 44 main bosses and 4 mini-bosses that have been discovered. This list is still a work in progress, so more bosses may be added or revealed in future updates. Bosses can be found across the many different locations in the game world, and it is common to encounter several bosses while exploring new areas. For a full overview of these locations, you can check the Locations page for detailed maps and descriptions.
There are also a few exclusive bosses that only appear in Steel Soul Mode, such as the Summoned Saviour, which offers an extra challenge for players looking for a harder experience.

The Hunter's Journal is given by Nuu in Greymoor (Halfway Home). There are 237 total
entries. Most enemies respawn when you sit on a bench or restart the game, except for
bosses and special enemies. To earn the first reward you must defeat 100 different
enemies. The entries for Shakra, Garmond & Zaza, Palestag, Lost Garmond, Lost Lace, and
Summoned Saviour are NOT required for the True Hunter achievement (the Summoned Saviour
is Steel Soul Mode only). After completing all required entries, talk to Nuu again to

is Steel Soul Mode only). After completing all required entries, talk to Nuu again to
receive the Hunter's Memento and unlock the achievement.
Entries marked with a note have a special spawn or unlock condition.

TIP: Practice the Hit and Run Tactic
General combat in Silksong revolves around a hit-and-run tactic: hit the enemy at least
once, then immediately move away to reposition. Beginners should practice this on regular
mobs until they get used to the rhythm of combat. This applies to most encounters, normal
enemies and bosses alike. Staying alive matters more than dealing damage, and practicing
caution even in the early game helps with harder challenges later.
 
 
TIP: Run Into Walls and Open Every Switch
When exploring, check every wall and open every switch for possible secrets. Running into

TIP: Run Into Walls and Open Every Switch
When exploring, check every wall and open every switch for possible secrets. Running into
walls can reveal hidden rooms containing items, secret paths, or Rosaries, and opening
switches usually removes exploration obstacles like locked gates and elevators. If
something can break, it is probably meant to break; this often applies to walls and vines
that open shortcuts, which is helpful when revisiting an area later.
 
 
TIP: Heal or Bind Only When Necessary
Binding (healing) fully depletes your Silk. While useful, it leaves you vulnerable because

TIP: Heal or Bind Only When Necessary
Binding (healing) fully depletes your Silk. While useful, it leaves you vulnerable because
you are trading health for ability use until Silk recharges. Since the early game has few
uses for Silk, use it only when necessary or when you can quickly replenish it. You can
also be interrupted while Binding: an interruption cancels the heal but still fully
depletes the Silk. Find a safe spot or moment in combat where completing a Bind is
guaranteed.
 
 
TIP: Use the Cocoon as Emergency Silk

depletes the Silk. Find a safe spot or moment in combat where completing a Bind is
guaranteed.
 
 
TIP: Use the Cocoon as Emergency Silk
When Hornet dies, she leaves behind a Cocoon at her death location and respawns at the
last bench rested on. Breaking this Cocoon restores Hornet's Silk (and the Rosaries lost
from the previous death) for healing or abilities. Instead of breaking it immediately,
treat the Cocoon as an extra spool of Silk. This is especially useful in later boss
encounters, where the extra Silk can provide an emergency heal or ability use.

encounters, where the extra Silk can provide an emergency heal or ability use.
 
 
TIP: Use Rosary Strings to Avoid Losing Rosaries
Rosaries are the main currency in Silksong and are lost on death. You can avoid losing
them by using Rosary Strings collected in Pharloom, or by paying a fee to certain vendors
to secure Rosaries to a string. Strung Rosaries cannot be spent without breaking the
string, so use this when expecting long expeditions without frequent vendor visits, or
when tackling unknown areas or difficult platforming sections.
 
 
TIP: Acquire the Maps ASAP

when tackling unknown areas or difficult platforming sections.
 
 
TIP: Acquire the Maps ASAP
Getting an area's Map as soon as possible is a necessity when exploring somewhere new.
Locate Shakra when entering a new area and purchase all the map necessities to keep track
of your progress.

Silksong is a large Metroidvania, so it is easy to get lost. Pharloom has multiple biomes
that hide secret paths and items. This guide gives a recommended route for each act,
focusing on the critical path toward the end of each act and the end of the game, while
noting where you can branch off to explore.

ACT 1 RECOMMENDED ROUTE

Recommended order for straight progression (not including optional Crests or Tools):
1. Moss Grotto
2. Bone Bottom
3. The Marrow
4. Deep Docks
5. Far Fields
6. Greymoor
7. Bellhart
8. Shellwood
9. Blasted Steps

You can enter Wormways early if you have a Simple Key, but it is much easier to explore
once you have the Cling Grip Ancestral Art, so do it after beating Sister Splinter at the
earliest.

You can reach Hunter's March as early as The Marrow, but you cannot progress through it
without the Drifter's Cloak, and the enemies and boss there are much easier once you have
Cling Grip.

Weavenest Atla is a very small area where you can upgrade your Hunter Crest, but it is
only accessible after defeating Widow, which you should do at the end of Shellwood (you
naturally loop back to Bellhart after getting Cling Grip).

The Slab is entirely optional at this point and is actually closer to The Citadel than the
rest of Pharloom. You may be captured by a bug in Pharloom, but you only have to explore
this jail area much later.

DECISION: Should I go through Blasted Steps or Sinner's Road?
Blasted Steps is the recommended way to reach the end of Act 1. It is west of Shellwood
and is much easier to explore than Sinner's Road. Sinner's Road is an alternate, harder
path that takes you through The Mist and the Exhaust Organ; you get a new Silk Skill for
defeating the boss inside the Organ, but The Mist is unpleasant. However, the boss at the
end of Blasted Steps is tricky and has a nasty run-back you will repeat often, so weigh
that trade-off.

ACT 2 RECOMMENDED ROUTE

ACT 2 RECOMMENDED ROUTE

In Act 2 the world opens up with more traversal tools and Ancestral Arts. Recommended
order around The Citadel for normal progression:
1. Choral Chambers
2. First Shrine / Songclave
3. Cogwork Core (bottom half)
4. Whispering Vaults (for the Vaultkeeper's Melody)
5. Underworks (for the Clawline Ancestral Art)
6. Mount Fay (for Double Jump / Faydown Cloak)
7. Cogwork Core (top half, for the Architect's Melody)
8. High Halls (for the Conductor's Melody)
9. The Cradle - Final Boss

Variant: if you entered the Grand Gate and started Act 2 from the Blasted Steps, you
automatically end up in the Underworks first; make your way through there before returning
to the Choral Chambers and heading to the First Shrine.

After you defeat the Cogwork Dancers (west of the First Shrine), you receive a quest to
acquire the Threefold Melody. As soon as you get the Vaultkeeper's Melody, go get the next
two traversal skills, Clawline and the Faydown Cloak (Double Jump); these two blow the rest
of the map wide open.

If you have not yet explored The Slab (because you were not captured in Act 1), do it on
the way to Mount Fay. After climbing the snowy mountain, Memorium, Sands of Karak, and
Wisp Thicket all become accessible. Whiteward is another optional area you can do whenever
(non-essential, though a Wish eventually takes you there).

DECISION: Which Threefold Melody should I get first?
Recommended order:
1. Vaultkeeper's Melody - needs no additional skills, so the Whispering Vaults is the
 easiest starting point (you must defeat a boss on the way).
2. Architect's Melody - needs at least Clawline to reach the top of the Cogwork Core;
 grabbing Double Jump first is also useful for the puzzle.
3. Conductor's Melody - the High Halls requires both Clawline and Double Jump, and this is
 put last because it may be one of the hardest challenges in the game: a ten-wave combat

put last because it may be one of the hardest challenges in the game: a ten-wave combat
 trial. Bring as many Mask Shards, Spool Fragments, and Needle Upgrades as you can.

Once you have all three melodies, talk to the trio of statues in the middle of the Cogwork
Core and play their songs; the elevator then opens to the final two bosses (for the normal
ending).

Bilewater is a totally optional area unless you are going for the true ending, so defeat
the final boss before going to Bilewater. For the True Ending and Act 3, see the separate
Act 3 unlock guide.

ACT 3 RECOMMENDED ROUTE

Act 3 starts with you compromised. Enemies are more dangerous, paths are blocked, and some
friends may no longer be friends.

After escaping The Cradle, you wake in Songclave with a new goal: free Pharloom from the
Void. Head to the Moss Grotto first; you must fight a boss before you can ride the Bell
Beast again. After Moss Grotto, head to The Abyss via Deep Docks, where you get an
important Silk Skill (Silk Soar). Back in Moss Grotto, you are given a new Needolin song and
a task to gather three Old Hearts. You can do these immediately, but it is highly
recommended to first max out health by collecting all Mask Shards, getting every Spool

recommended to first max out health by collecting all Mask Shards, getting every Spool
Fragment, and fully upgrading your Needle. After getting the three Hearts, return to the
Shamans, head back to Deep Docks, re-enter the Void, and fight the final boss.

DECISION: What's the best order to collect the Old Hearts in?
You can get all three in any order; each is challenging in different ways and requires the
Elegy for the Deep song on the Needolin to access. Preferred order:
1. Heart of the Woods - by far the easiest, but the most hidden. Return to the collapsed
 lift shaft just west of the Underworks, use Silk Soar to find a broken wall on the left.
 Two bosses here, but easier than the other trials; the second is a gimmick boss, so
 bring long-range Tools.

Two bosses here, but easier than the other trials; the second is a gimmick boss, so
 bring long-range Tools.
2. Heart of Might - at the Coral Tower (Sands of Karak, where you fought the Vengeful
 Conchfly). Climb a battle tower and win three stages: 5 waves, then 10, then 15. A
 shortcut after the last phase leads to a shorter trial and a boss. The boss may be the
 easiest of the three, but the battle tower makes it harder to reach.

easiest of the three, but the battle tower makes it harder to reach.
3. Heart of the Wild - held by one of the hardest bosses in the game, which is why it is
 recommended last. Access it through Hunter's March using Silk Soar by the statue of the
 Ant's leader, unlocking a new section of Far Fields. A few easy combat trials precede
 the boss; the boss is not easy.

Secret fourth Old Heart: if you freed the Green Prince in Sinner's Road, you gain access to
Verdania (a small area east of Greymoor). Defeat the boss there for the Conjoined Heart,
which can substitute for any one of the three Hearts. Even if you do not use it to access
the final boss, the fight is highly recommended.

Following this guide on a new playthrough leaves you able to fully explore every area
accessible before leaving Act 1 and starting Act 2. The ability order covered is:
Silkspear, fast travel (Bell Beast), Swift Step, Drifter's Cloak, Cling Grip, and Needolin.

BEGINNER BASICS (TUTORIAL TO BONE BOTTOM)

BEGINNER BASICS (TUTORIAL TO BONE BOTTOM)

Bind to your pin and use basic abilities to reach the first bench. Explore a little for free
Shell Shards and Rosaries, the game's two currencies. Shell Shards are a lesser currency
used as donations for certain quests and to refill offensive tools found later. Rosaries are
the main currency for buying from vendors and are dropped on death; you reacquire them by
returning to where you died and striking the cocoon, but dying a second time before
recovering them loses them forever.

The first bench is in the Ruined Chapel. Benches restore health, manually save the game, and
set your respawn point. Just ahead is the first boss, the Moss Mother. Use Hornet's dive to
get extra attacks, and build silk with successful strikes; when the silk meter is full and
glowing, hold the input to restore up to 3 health. Getting struck mid-heal causes damage,
fails the heal, and loses the silk being used, so only heal when you have a few safe seconds.

After defeating the Moss Mother, go west and up the large bead rope, then east; a cutscene
and an NPC give you The Great Citadel objective, the primary Act 1 goal. Continue east into
Bone Bottom, the first town, and rest at the bench. Speak with the inhabitants (one is a
merchant), then head east into The Marrow.

MAPS AND THE SHAKRA NPC (THE MARROW)

Move through The Marrow going up and east toward the boss chamber. Several enemies spawn
there and must all be defeated to drop into a new chamber. Move west to unlock a gate back to
Bone Bottom, then east past an offering bowl that can be paid (Rosaries) to unlock a fancy
bench (optional). Continue east, then up and west, to a chamber with temporary platforms;
scale them to the top-west corner and follow the path toward an NPC singing. Hit a small
lever to open the way, grab free Rosaries and Shell Shards, and meet Shakra, who sells your

lever to open the way, grab free Rosaries and Shell Shards, and meet Shakra, who sells your
first maps and map-making supplies. Maps and the compass aren't strictly necessary but make
navigation much easier.

OPTIONAL EARLY ROSARY FARM (NEAR SHAKRA / MOSSHOME)
Up and west of Shakra is a path to the Mosshome zone. Move west along the elevated platform
past projectile-shooting bugs (their projectiles can be destroyed), hit a lever to open a
path below, drop in, and move east on the elevated section to a secluded chamber with a
bench; a lever here opens a shortcut back to Shakra. Farming loop: from this bench, move west
into Mosshome, defeat two easy enemies (5 Rosaries each), drop to the bottom floor for 3 more

into Mosshome, defeat two easy enemies (5 Rosaries each), drop to the bottom floor for 3 more
enemies toward the bottom-east exit, then loop back to the bench. You get about 25 Rosaries
every 30 seconds. Equip the Magnetite Brooch if you have it.

SILKSPEAR (FIRST ABILITY)

From Shakra, move up and west into Moss Grotto, then to the top-west corner into a new
chamber. Move far east avoiding traps, then up and west to a weaver altar; inspecting it
unlocks the Silkspear. It costs silk to use and cuts through thick webbing to open new areas
(for example, a Frayed Rosary String to the west). Dropping down and moving east leads to
more webs; to the east is a bench, plus a Shaman NPC who gives the Berry Picking Wish
(optional side quest).
FAST TRAVEL (BELL BEAST BOSS, SILK HEART)

West of the Shaman, drop down and move west back toward a previous chamber, then to Shakra (a
bench here makes a closer spawn point for the boss). Free the Bell Beast with the Silkspear,
then defeat it. Its attacks deal 2 damage each but it has only 3 telegraphed moves, signaled
by the ground rumble before it emerges:
- Medium-height, wide plume: it charges (easiest time to deal damage).
- Wide but much taller plume: a leap attack; stay under it, wait for the landing, then strike.

- Wide but much taller plume: a leap attack; stay under it, wait for the landing, then strike.
- Narrow plume: it throws bouncing bells out to the sides (hard to punish; watch the bells).
Phase 2 is similar but adds an extra bell drop on each of the 3 major moves.

After the fight, interact with the floating orb, do a short platforming section, then a
second orb to Consume a Silk Heart (small regenerative silk capacity). Return to the Bellways
and jump up-right to unlock your first fast-travel post (to/from Bone Bottom). Strike the
lever in the next chamber east to unlock a bench and the path to a new region.

SWIFT STEP (THE MARROW PRISON TO DEEP DOCKS)

Start at the bench just east of The Marrow bellways. Move east through The Marrow (spike
pits, crumbling platforms). In the second horizontal chamber you can reach an upper area by
dive-attacking off bouncy red flowers, but the bottom route works; drop down a few levels and
follow the sign east into a jail with a free bench. From there go down and east into Deep
Docks (watch for spike pits, falling rocks, and lava). Follow Shakra's singing for the Deep
Docks Map (a sign-posted bench here costs Rosaries and is skippable).

To get Swift Step, head up and east through the major portion of Deep Docks. Exit at the
northeast corner (hit a lever to reach it). In the next chamber move east, jumping along hot
coal surfaces to avoid burn damage, then hit an explosive core to open the path. There is
another purchasable bench here worth considering before the upcoming enemy-wave encounter.
From that bench, go back west, then up and east to a narrow corridor; moving west reaches the
boss chamber with multiple enemy waves. Afterward, move west and drop into a new chamber,

boss chamber with multiple enemy waves. Afterward, move west and drop into a new chamber,
watch for spike pits, strike the explosive rock at the southeast corner for a shortcut back,
then take the southwest exit and reach an altar; interact to unlock Swift Step.

DRIFTER'S CLOAK (FLEXIBLE SPINES QUEST)

From the Swift Step altar in Deep Docks, move west toward Shakra. (Optional, recommended:
follow the bench sign and drop into a nearby chamber, clear a boss room of enemy waves to
unlock a free bench near the next boss and a vendor selling the powerful Sting Shard tool.)
From the free bench, move up to the previous chamber and use Swift Step to open the boss
chamber to the east, where you fight Lace, a nimble foe that teaches the dodge function of
Swift Step. After Lace, move east and strike the lever to unlock a bench and gate.

Move east into Far Fields to the Bellways station (defeat humanoid foes along the way for
Rosaries if you have fewer than 30). Use the Sprint and air-dash functions of Swift Step to
stay on the upper path; exit top-east to the Far Fields Bellways and useful bench. (Optional
Far Fields Rosary farm: from the bench, go west, cross the top platforms past two explosive
clusters, drop and defeat the Skarr Scout west for 13 Rosaries, then Silkspear the paired
Skarr Scout and Skarr Stalker east for just over 50 Rosaries total; save and quit to return

Skarr Scout and Skarr Stalker east for just over 50 Rosaries total; save and quit to return
to the bench and repeat.)

From the Far Fields Bellways, move west and drop down hugging the eastern wall to reach
Shakra for the Far Fields Map. Then move west, drop, and head east (watch the lava) through a
long chamber, then up to the Hut of the Seamstress (free bench). Speaking with the Seamstress
gives the Flexible Spines quest, required for the Drifter's Cloak. Move down and east to find
Hokers: strike one, avoid the spines it shoots, then strike the loose spines to harvest them.
You need 25 Spine Cores. Three Hokers are easily accessed here; leave and re-enter the

You need 25 Spine Cores. Three Hokers are easily accessed here; leave and re-enter the
chamber to respawn them and repeat until you have 25. Return to the Seamstress's Hut and turn
them in to receive the Drifter's Cloak, which hovers in midair and rides updraft shafts found
throughout Pharloom.

CLING GRIP (FOURTH CHORUS BOSS, THEN SHELLWOOD AND SISTER SPLINTER)

From the Seamstress's Hut, move west back toward the Far Fields Bellways. The Fourth Chorus
boss interrupts the trip and is a chance to test the Drifter's Cloak: use it to avoid
horizontal swipes, and in the final phase ride the air streams on either side to hit the two
explosive cores, instantly ending the fight.

Go west, ride the air streams back to the Bellways bench, then ride a series of air streams
upward through the large vertical tube, striking explosive cores to open the way. At a closed
metal gate, follow the bench sign east (a paid structure with a vendor locks again on exit,
so buy everything on the first visit). Move east, loop up and west along the top to get above
the gate, hit the lever to open it, then ascend the tube past more cores and a lever, arriving
in Greymoor. East of here is Shakra (Greymoor map) and a free bench.

Move west through Greymoor toward the next zone. Past a cave of pilgrim-style enemies is the
Halfway Home (free bench); upstairs, speak with Nuu for the quest to defeat Pharloom's bugs
and the Hunter's Journal to track them. Continue west to a small arena (easy enemies), then
unlock shortcuts before the Moorwing boss, since you may run back several times. In the tower
structure, go up past scissor-wielding bugs and a spinning wheel, take the lower of two west

structure, go up past scissor-wielding bugs and a spinning wheel, take the lower of two west
doors, descend and break a shortcut, drop down, hit a lever east for a major shortcut, then go
west and follow the town signs to the Moorwing.

MOORWING BOSS TIPS
A very difficult boss (took the guide author 10+ attempts). In phase two, all attacks get
substantial upgrades plus one new move:
- The flurry of leg attacks is NOT upgraded, so it remains a top opportunity to deal damage.
- A new move launches two blades simultaneously; stay under Moorwing and time jumps to also
 deal damage.
- The swoop attack is now followed by a single blade launch.
- The blade-launch attack now uses two blades; the second curves up earlier and circles back
 down to strike the ground. This is the most dangerous move, so get far away when telegraphed.

down to strike the ground. This is the most dangerous move, so get far away when telegraphed.
Note: this fight does not occur here if you completed the Flea questline and traveled with
them to arrive for the first time.

After Moorwing, move west into Haunted Bellhart, rest at the bench (note: quitting while
sitting here temporarily traps you in silk strands on reload). Move west into Shellwood,
ascend the large chamber in an S shape to the top-west exit, then drop west into a boss
chamber of enemy waves (the stickbugs are worst; store silk and hit them with Silkspears).
Go west and up to a purchasable bench (60 Rosaries) that serves as a checkpoint for two boss

Go west and up to a purchasable bench (60 Rosaries) that serves as a checkpoint for two boss
fights. Down and west of the bench, ride a lift down to Shakra for the Bellhart and Shellwood
maps. Then go up the platforming section (nail-bounce off white flowers) to the top-east
exit, up and east through the next exit, then activate the lift near the top-east exit as a
shortcut back to the bench.

To reach Sister Splinter, ride the lift up, take the eastern exit, and platform to the
top-west exit into the boss chamber. Sister Splinter herself isn't too hard: destroy the
splinters she makes to keep room to maneuver and hit her. The danger is the adds she spawns,
best removed with Silkspears or ranged tools. After defeating her, move west, drop briefly,
then up to the top-west exit; in the next chamber move west, break vines, and needle-pogo up
to the top-east exit to reach the altar that unlocks Cling Grip (wall jump).

NEEDOLIN (WIDOW BOSS)

Start at the bench near Sister Splinter. Ride the elevator east up, or wall-jump up. Move
through the northeast passage, then all the way east; access a new area by wall-jumping up to
piles of bells and smacking through them. Move down and east past new enemy types, then west
and down; between two winged furms is a hidden breakable wall. Beyond it is a hole - jump over
it for now and move west through another hidden wall to open a shortcut back toward the bench
by breaking vines at the west end.

Heal at the bench if needed, return east through the last chamber, drop down a couple of holes
and move east to the Widow boss. Widow is comparable to Moorwing in difficulty; not all
attacks deal double damage but enough do, and it's a "death by a thousand cuts" fight. Phase
two is frantic at first but more predictable: Widow drops vertical or diagonal bells a few
times, then does a flurry attack; once you learn the rhythm, phase two gets easier. After
defeating Widow, plunge your needle and bind to unlock the Needolin. A short platforming

defeating Widow, plunge your needle and bind to unlock the Needolin. A short platforming
section exits a dream sequence; after waking, hit the nearby lever to unlock a bench and free
Bellhart town from its curse.

More mobility and traversal items exist, but only after starting Act 2. For now you can fully
explore Act 1; if you can't access an area, it's because it requires Act 2 progression.

This is a guide trying to summarize what is required for 100% in Hollow Knight: Silksong. While its mostly completed, it is still being updated based on feedbacks received on comments.

Apologies if there's already a guide like this, but I couldn't find it, which made my 100% run a lot more complex that it was likely needed.

And since probably more people are starting to finish the game and will start wondering what's needed for the 100% completion and what isn't, and the game doesn't let it clear as far as I know, so I'll try to list the requirements below. If I forgot any, feel free to correct me in comments!

REQUIRED

All Crests

All 57 Tools (which also means all Craftmetals)

All Crafting Kit Upgrades

All side quests (board quests and NPC quests) that gives new tools, masks, fragments, upgrades, or ar required for story progression

Binding Eva (sources I could find as of now mention having finding all 7 optional Crests + 15 memory lockets as the requirements for it. Iirc, there are 20 lockets in total. Try using the lockets as well)

All Silk Skills

All ability upgrades (i.e double jump, etc)

All fleas (required for Egg of Fleelia tool)

All Masks

All Spool Fragments

All Needle Upgrades (uncertain on this one so I'll leave it last, but you need the quests that gives the 3 Oils anyways)

All Needle Upgrades (uncertain on this one so I'll leave it last, but you need the quests that gives the 3 Oils anyways)

Everbloom (acquired from the snails, needed to fight the final boss. Counts as an ability)

NOT REQUIRED

Cursed Ending

Tool Upgrades (some tools can be upgraded)

Minigames (except the Flea one for the questline)

Bellheart Delivieries (except the one for the Pale Oil quest)

Housing Furniture

Hunter Log (Bestiary, whatever you may call it)

Mementos (accessed from insideyour Bellhome)

Housing Furniture

Hunter Log (Bestiary, whatever you may call it)

Mementos (accessed from insideyour Bellhome)

Materium (accessed from insideyour Bellhome)

Any NPC dialogues/chains that don't result in a "required" item (i.e Seth questline)

Any Hidden Boss that don't result in a "required" item (i.e Crawfather, Shakra, etc)

Any hidden map zone, segment or shortcut that doesn't result in a "required item"

Any hidden map zone, segment or shortcut that doesn't result in a "required item"

Extra Arenas (some arenas reset in act 3 and need to be done again with cursed enemy variants, but there are usually ways around them and they usually don't give required items for 100%)

General Tips

Quitting to the menu puts you back at the last bench you rested (outside of after acquiring new abilities, as those counts as checkpoints).

Quitting to the menu puts you back at the last bench you rested (outside of after acquiring new abilities, as those counts as checkpoints).

While Crawfather isn't needed, it gives an item that passively generates Rosaries once installed outside your Bellhome, so he should be killed ASAP. It could remove any potential Rosary farming you'd need to.

Leftmost bench in Citadel below the High Halls is a great place to farm Rosaries in Act 3 (6k+ per hour). Run to the bench, kill the 3 enemies, return, repeat. Use the super speed tool to speed it up. The long hall in Whiteward also seems to be a great place, offering up to 10k/h (requires quitting to the menu after every run).

Shards aren't consumed for tools used inside dream sections (accessed with the song in Act 3). Those are great tool testing grounds.

Shards aren't consumed for tools used inside dream sections (accessed with the song in Act 3). Those are great tool testing grounds.

Can convert Rosaries into Shards at the Deep Docks merchant (buy shard packs for 50 Rosaries). Other merchants like the ones at Bellheart and Songclave (possibly some others as well) also offer those.

There are 53 Tools in the game. The best of them make Hornet far deadlier, and many provide
synergies when equipped together, opening up buildcrafting opportunities.

RED TOOLS (active, offensive)

Red Tools are active and offensive. Each costs its usage bar to activate; when the bar is
depleted the Tool becomes unusable until refreshed by spending Shell Shards at a resting
bench. With the Architect Crest, a specific input refreshes a Tool's bar on the go.

Sting Shard - Places a small spike trap in the air; when enemies touch it the spikes extend
and deal solid damage. A bit more expensive to use than some damaging Tools, but its
early-game availability via the merchant Forge Daughter makes it a strong pick. Littering an
arena with Sting Shards works well against bosses in close quarters.

Flea Brew - Increases your movement and attack speed for a short duration with no other
effects. One use vastly bumps up damage potential, especially with high-attack-speed Crests
like the Beast or Wanderer. The movement-speed boost is jarring at first but makes moving
around in a fight much easier once you adjust.

Delver's Drill - As long as you are above the enemy, one of the best burst-damage Tools in
the game, even more so with an upgraded Tool pouch. It shoots Hornet down at high speed and
drills into enemies up to five times on contact. Can kill most trash mobs in a single use
and makes tanky bosses less laborious.

Silkshot - Obtained from the shop near the top of Mount Fay; effectively a railgun. Fires a
laser beam that instantly reaches maximum range, ignores enemy armour, and pierces through
multiple foes. Hornet briefly hangs in the air when firing, so it doubles as a platforming
tool alongside its high damage.

Flintslate - "Superheats" your Needle, increasing its damage output by a full 50% for a
brief duration. Like Binding and the Silkshot, using it midair makes Hornet hang, which is
useful for avoiding a collision with an enemy on your descent.

BLUE TOOLS (passive buffs)

BLUE TOOLS (passive buffs)

Blue Tools provide passive buffs and bonuses when equipped, and can change or improve how you
use Silk, particularly the damage and effects of Silk Skills. There are no drawbacks, but no
Crest has more than three Blue slots, so you can never equip all of them at once.

Injector Band - Makes Binding to heal take less time. The faster heal saves you from wasting
Silk by getting hit mid-Bind, which happens at least a few times per playthrough.

Multibinder - Splits the Binding heal animation into two parts, each Bind healing two Masks,
for four health restored total. Binding takes almost twice as long; mitigate this by pairing
it with the Injector Band, whose effects complement it.

Volt Filament - Adds a pink lightning effect that drastically improves the damage of all Silk
Skills, plus a brief minor damage-over-time effect. Valuable in the later game as enemy
health pools grow. (Obtained by killing the big shocking enemy near the Sands of Karak path -
i.e. Voltvyrm.)

Claw Mirrors - A Binding-centric Tool (single and dual versions). When you Bind to heal, it
causes a sparkling explosion around Hornet that damages nearby enemies. Works with the
Multibinder (two explosions in quick succession). With the Warding Bell equipped, that Tool's
damage-mitigation effect makes the Mirror explosion much larger.

Longclaw - Extends the range of your Needle regardless of equipped Crest, helping with
attacks that fall just short. Especially appreciated on short-range Crests like the Wanderer,
but longer ones like the active Beast Crest benefit too. The extra range lets you keep tough
bosses at arm's length while still dealing damage. (Reward for completing the Broodfeast quest
for the Huntress.)

YELLOW TOOLS (utility and quality-of-life)

Yellow Tools are a catch-all: some give buffs and bonuses, others offer quality-of-life
exploration features, and a select few have powerful battle-turning effects.

Compass - Shows Hornet's current location on the map (provided the area map is unlocked), and
her icon moves with her as she explores with the map open. Not strictly necessary, but saves
a few seconds whenever you'd otherwise guess your position in Pharloom.

Weighted Belt - Greatly reduces knockback from enemy attacks (some knockback remains). Helps
Hornet withstand hits more efficiently and avoid being pushed into hazards, letting you stand
your ground or keep up an offensive without bouncing around the arena.

Magnetite Dice - Gives a small chance to negate a single hit entirely (no damage), though you
still take the knockback and gain the invincibility frames. Pairs reasonably with the
Weighted Belt. Luck-based, so don't rely on it, but it can save an otherwise lost fight.

Ascendant's Grip - After getting the Cling Grip ability, this Tool lets you stick to walls
without sliding down, so you can line up your next jump more carefully. Makes trickier
platforming sections much less frustrating.
